title: Explainer: hazard ratio + proportional-hazards assumption (highest-misread)
context: The hazard ratio is the single most commonly misread statistic in oncology trial reports. Clinicians routinely interpret a HR of 0.70 as '30% lower risk over the whole study period', when in fact a HR is an instantaneous, time-averaged relative hazard under a proportional-hazards assumption — not a simple risk ratio and not a constant risk reduction over time. When the proportional-hazards assumption fails (crossing survival curves), the HR becomes a misleading single-number summary and restricted mean survival time (RMST) is a more honest alternative. This explainer is the highest-priority pilot explainer for M0 and must be biostatistician-signed-off and clinical-framing-reviewed before release. It depends on the authoring template (template-002), the licensing/data gate (datagate-003), and the reproducibility harness (repro-005).
objective: Author, biostatistician-sign-off, and publish the hazard ratio explainer — covering the precise definition, the 'not a simple risk ratio' common trap, the proportional-hazards assumption and how to check it, and an introduction to RMST as an alternative — with a fully reproducible worked example on open/synthetic data, complete provenance, and a structural 'not clinical advice' frame.
acceptanceCriteria: The explainer provides both a plain definition (colleague-to-colleague) and a precise definition of a hazard ratio (instantaneous relative hazard, time-averaged under the proportional-hazards model).

An explicit commonTrap section corrects the most common misreading: HR is not a simple risk ratio; it is not a constant 'X% lower risk over the whole period'; it is an instantaneous, time-averaged relative hazard. Each trap entry includes misreading, why_wrong, and correct_reading.
The proportional-hazards assumption is stated in assumptionsAndLimits with a description of how to check it (e.g. log-log plots, Schoenfeld residuals) and what RMST offers when it fails.

A worked example is built on an open/synthetic dataset with a data manifest entry; every number and figure in the example is emitted by a committed script, diff-matched by the reproducibility harness (repro-005), and checksummed in provenance.generatedFiguresChecksum.
Every conceptual claim cites an authoritative methods source (peer-reviewed biostatistics, recognized textbook, or reporting guideline) in the claims[] array; no uncited statistical claim is present.
A howToReadInAPaper checklist is included (checkable items a clinician can apply when reading a trial reporting a HR).
Figures have complete alt-text; figures use colorblind-safe palettes; readability target is met for a clinician/trainee audience.
A structural 'educational, not clinical advice' framing field is present; no treatment-advice or patient-specific content appears.
Biostatistician statsSignoff is recorded in the review block; clinical-framing review is passed; plain-language/accessibility review is passed.
Zero stated statistical falsehoods: every definition, trap correction, and assumption is verified by the biostatistician reviewer.
pnpm build && pnpm test && pnpm lint pass on any committed tooling; commit is DCO signed-off.
resources: tasks/stats-for-clinicians-datagate-003.json — data gate and manifest for the worked example dataset
tasks/stats-for-clinicians-repro-005.json — reproducibility harness for worked-example figures
PLAN.md — Solution approach authoring pipeline; Data/licensing section
Reporting guidelines: CONSORT, SAMPL (statistical reporting guidelines), ICH E9
Recognized survival-analysis methods literature (cited per-claim, not copied)
Open/synthetic example data: synthetic survival dataset or license-verified open teaching survival dataset (manifest entry required)
output: A published, biostatistician-signed-off hazard ratio explainer committed to the project repo, with 100% reproducible worked example, complete provenance, every claim cited, accessibility passed, and a structural 'not clinical advice' frame. This is the M0 pilot explainer with the highest impact on correcting clinician misreading.
